coredata.dc:title: Single optical sensor to multiple functions: Ratiometric sensing for SO3 2− and dual signal determination for copper (II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Nengtao
Wen, Qin
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots possess superior emissive features, but most of them give rise to luminescence bands in the blue region. In addition, blue or green emissions have difficulty in penetrating tissue deeply. Therefore, long wavelength emissive signals are welcome for the development and application of polymeric dots towards sensing and bio-analysis. Herein, the color-tunable fluorescence polymer nanoparticles (F-PNPs) have been synthesized via one-step strategy based on the employment of hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine as precursors at low temperature. Moreover, its emission peak can be shifted from 523 nm to 612 nm by varying the excitation wavelength in the range of 380 nm to 480 nm. In view of sensing assessment, F-PNPs enable the quantitative determination of trace amount of SO3 2− and Cu2+. In the presence of SO3 2−, the polymer dots exhibit ratiometric fluorescence signals in deionized water and the color change from green to blue has been clearly observed by naked eyes (detection limit = 59 nM). In addition, two emission bands at 545 nm (green) and 601 nm (red) are observed to be responsive to the exposure of Cu2+. The entire dual sensing system for the detection of Cu2+ will be more accurate and reliable. The evaluation results reveal their optical signals are improved linearly due to the addition of Cu2+ at increasing concentrations and the detection limits are calculated to be 76 nM (green) and 41 nM (red), respectively. Such polymeric network will provide a new dynamic platform for sensing purposes in biomedicine study, environmental protection, and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Photoluminescence
Color change
Multifunctional
Ratiometric

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and pH stability study
Detection of copper ions and sodium sulfite
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of SO3 2− in aqueous solution
Dual fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ in aqueous solution
Determination of copper ions in laboratory tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of F-PNPs (Inset: Diameter distribution histogram image of F-PNPs) and (b) DLS curve of F-PNPs.
FT-IR spectra of Hydroquinone and the synthesized F-PNPs.
UV–vis absorption (red), PL excitation (blue) and emission spectra (green) of F-PNPs.

(a) Emission spectra of F-PNPs, F-PNPs + SO3 2− and F-PNPs in the pH = 14; (b) Emission spectra of F-PNPs after the addition of SO3 2− from 0 to 50 μM; (c) Linear plot of titration spectra (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the F-PNPs in the presence and absence of SO3 2−, respectively) and (d) Graph of the fluorescence peak intensity of F-PNPs with alternated dipping into HCl.

(a) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 545 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+; (b) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 545 nm with the addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 70 μM; (c) Linear plot of titration spectra (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the F-PNPs in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively) and (d) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 545 nm in the presence of various ions.

(a) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 601 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+; (b) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 601 nm with the addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 45 μM; (c) Linear plot of titration spectra (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the F-PNPs in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively) and (d) Emission spectra of F-PNPs at 601 nm in the presence of various ions.
Decay curves of (a) F-PNPs and F-PNPs/SO3 2−, (b) Emission spectra of F-PNPs/Cu2+ at 545 nm and (c) Emission spectra of F-PNPs/Cu2+ at 601 nm.
TEM images of (a) F-PNPs, (b) F-PNPs/SO3 2− and (c) F-PNPs/Cu2+.
Schematic illustration of F-PNPs synthesis and dual sensing process of Cu2+ and SO3 2−.
Analytical results of SO3 2− in top water samples by ratiometric fluorescence sensing method.

head.ce:title: Single optical sensor to multiple functions: Ratiometric sensing for SO3 2− and dual signal determination for copper (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence polymer nanoparticles (F-PNPs) were synthesized via one-pot strategy from hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine. The as-prepared F-PNPs display superior aqueous dispersibility, excellent photo-and chemical stability and excitation wavelength dependence. Its green luminescence could be responsive to SO3 2− and the quenching mechanism was discussed. Cu2+ could lead to the quenching effects at both 545 nm and 601 nm, thus providing a dual channel detection mode. To our knowledge, it will be the first example for the ratiometric sensing of SO3 2− and double sensing modes of Cu2+.

Polymer dots possess superior emissive features, but most of them give rise to luminescence bands in the blue region. In addition, blue or green emissions have difficulty in penetrating tissue deeply. Therefore, long wavelength emissive signals are welcome for the development and application of polymeric dots towards sensing and bio-analysis. Herein, the color-tunable fluorescence polymer nanoparticles (F-PNPs) have been synthesized via one-step strategy based on the employment of hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine as precursors at low temperature. Moreover, its emission peak can be shifted from 523 nm to 612 nm by varying the excitation wavelength in the range of 380 nm to 480 nm. In view of sensing assessment, F-PNPs enable the quantitative determination of trace amount of SO3 2− and Cu2+. In the presence of SO3 2−, the polymer dots exhibit ratiometric fluorescence signals in deionized water and the color change from green to blue has been clearly observed by naked eyes (detection limit = 59 nM). In addition, two emission bands at 545 nm (green) and 601 nm (red) are observed to be responsive to the exposure of Cu2+. The entire dual sensing system for the detection of Cu2+ will be more accurate and reliable. The evaluation results reveal their optical signals are improved linearly due to the addition of Cu2+ at increasing concentrations and the detection limits are calculated to be 76 nM (green) and 41 nM (red), respectively. Such polymeric network will provide a new dynamic platform for sensing purposes in biomedicine study, environmental protection, and food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials, including organic molecules, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), sulfur dots, silver nanoparticles and semiconductors, have stimulated tremendous interests in the fields of bio-sensing and evaluation [1–7]. In recent years, fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (F-PNPs) have emerged as new alternatives and they are generally derived from the assembly of low molecular weight molecules via auto-polymerization [8], amidation reaction [9] and Schiff base reaction [10]. The achieved particles possess high water solubility, special optical property and good photo-stability. However, most of F-PNPs emit mainly in blue and green region and such short-wavelength emissions have limited penetration capability and will exert severe photo-damage problems to biological tissues. Therefore, it would not be suitable to employ the blue/green dots towards practical applications in fluorescent signal response and biological imaging [11–14]. In this way, the design of water-soluble, biocompatible, long-wavelength and multicolor emissive materials, will be expected and highly desirable.

As far as the analyte was concerned, the current developed ones were primarily based on single target determination, which demonstrated its feasibility and specificity for object recognition [15]. Few optical probes can identify two or more targets, but it is difficult to distinguish the signals, owing to limited separation ability. Hence, the realization of simultaneous detection of two guests with sensitivity and accuracy will be compulsory.

With the aim of exploring multiple targets sensing, the ratiometric fluorescent signal can be regarded as an effective strategy [16,17]. Such treatment would not only eliminate false signals caused by environmental effects, but also improve its accuracy and higher sensitivity [18]. Based on theory, the ratiometric curves can be accurately recorded for the changes in the ratios of fluorescent intensities at two wavelengths by an excitation wavelength [18,19]. Compared to the conventional organic probes [20,21], F-PNPs have enough solubility in water and better stability. In addition, the development of a dual-mode fluorescent probe with specific responses will be significant.

As for the selection of target species, sulfite has been regarded as a frequently-used food additive and it is a microbial inhibitor. In addition, such anion is an environmental pollutant released by industry equipment. The determination of sulfite would be valuable in a variety of fields. In view of nanoparticles, the incorporation of external species such as SO3 2− could cause notable observation that the apparent shifts including band edge absorption and emission are achieved. Its particle size and shape would be significantly changed and quantum confinement effect plays a major role in the recognition process. During electrical device production and battery fabrication, it would be hard to deal with the excessive amounts of copper ions. The accurate detection of Cu2+ could avoid possible damages to human health. In this contribution, the surface of F-PNPs would be covered by multiple nitrogen or oxygen atoms and the lonely-pair electrons could effectively participate into the coordination with Cu2+ ions. It has been well accepted that copper ions might lead to fluorescence quenching due to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) since N/O units acted as PET donors. Therefore, the specific determination of SO3 2− and Cu2+ would be highly expected.

Herein, we provided an effective and useful path to employ hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine as carbon source and nitrogen source to synthesize uniform small-sized F-PNPs without any chemical modification through one-step stirring method at low temperature. In this work, a nanoprobe (on–off) for Cu2+ based on two modes and a ratiometric sensor for SO3 2− were constructed. Upon incubation with SO3 2− in F-PNPs aqueous solution at room temperature, the emission intensity at 430 nm would increase and the fluorescence quenching at 540 nm was observed. Furthermore, the green and red emission profiles of F-PNPs were sensitive to the addition of copper ions. Double mode detection of Cu2+ under variable channels was achieved and such sensing platform was more indicative and accurate. To our knowledge, it will be the first time to report the determination of Cu2+ and SO3 2− via the new-developed polymeric dots. Therefore, the multiple target sensing capability would expand the application range. Scheme 1 illustrated the assembly procedure of F-PNPs and the determination pictures for the dual channel detection of Cu2+ and SO3 2−.

Hydroquinone (C6H6O2, 99%), polyethyleneimine ((CH2CH2NH)n, M.W.600, 99%; M.W.1800, 99%; M.W.10000, 99% and M.W.70000, 50% aqueous solution) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, AR, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. All the other chemicals were analytical reagents and used without further purification.

Fluorescence was recorded by using a spectrophotometer (F-4600) with the xenon lamp as the light source (Hitachi, Japan) respectively. All the scan speed was fixed at 1500 nm/min. Both excitation and emission slit widths were 10.0 nm. UV–visible spectrophotometer was collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Japan). TEM images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. Nanoparticles size was also obtained with dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Instrument, UK, Nano-ZS90). Decay curves were measured with a Transient/Stationary Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS-920, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The pH measurements were carried out with a digital pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland).

F-PNPs were prepared by one step. Briefly, 20 μL of polyethyleneimine and 22.0 mg hydroquinone were added into 25 mL ultrapure water and heated at 40 ℃ under magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then the color of solution was turned from colorless to reddish brown. The product was collected by filtration and large particles were removed. The obtained solution was purified via the employment of dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off~1000 Da) against the deionized water for 12 h. The F-PNPs powder was obtained by freeze-dried under vacuum for 48 h for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and pH stability study
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent emission and excitation spectra of F-PNPs (0.3 mg/ml) were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. To evaluate stability of F-PNPs under different pH values, its fluorescence spectra were evaluated by adjusting the pH values (pH = 3 to pH = 14) of the sensing system. In addition, the F-PNPs (0.3 mg/mL) at different pH values were prepared in aqueous solution and pH values were measured by pH meter under room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of copper ions and sodium sulfite
ce:section.ce:para: In the system of ions detection, F-PNPs were dispersed into 2 mL buffer solution to achieve the final concentration of 0.3 mg/mL. In the presence of copper ions at different concentrations, the fluorescence spectra of F-PNPs were measured. At the same experimental conditions, a variety of ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Li+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Pb2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2−, CO3 2–, PO4 3− and NO3 –) were added in 0.3 mg/mL F-PNPs solution and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

As given in Scheme 1, F-PNPs were synthesized by using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and hydroquinone (HQ) under homogeneously stirring for 30 min at low temperature. The photophysical property of F-PNPs might be correlated with several parameters including different molecular weight of PEI, the mass ratio between PEI and HQ, reaction temperature and time. Firstly, the influence of molecular weight of PEI (PEI600, PEI1800, PEI10000 and PEI70000) on the preparation of F-PNPs was studied and their emission curves were recorded. In the presence of PEI600, the fluorescence intensity of the F-PNPs was the most intensive as provided in Fig. S1a. Therefore, PEI600 was used for subsequent experiments. Secondly, the mass ratio range between HQ and PEI600 from 1 to 10 was variable in parallel reactions. The ratio of 6 was determined to be optimal due to its best fluorescence performance of F-PNPs (Fig. S1b) and it is not favorable to give better performance beyond the ratio range. Thirdly, the effect of reaction temperature (20℃, 30℃, 40℃, 50℃ and 60℃) to emission intensity was investigated. As the temperature was elevated, the reaction process has been generated. If the reaction speed was too fast, it will cause rapid aggregation of F-PNPs and reduce its fluorescence. As shown in Figure S1c, the photoluminescence intensity during reaction temperature at 40℃ was higher than other conditions. Afterward, the reaction time was controlled to be 30 min (Figure S1d), respectively. In this way, the parameter optimization for F-PNPs was accomplished and the sample can be suitable for following studies.

For the sake of verifying the structure and size distribution of F-PNPs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured as shown in Fig. 1 a. F-PNPs had highly monodisperse nature without aggregation and quasi-spherical shape. The inset in Fig. 1a supported that the size distribution of F-PNPs was narrow from 5.0 to 8.50 nm with the average diameter of 6.20 nm. In addition, the average hydrodynamic diameter from dynamic light scattering (DLS) in water was analogous to TEM results and the mean particle size was about 7.50 nm in Fig. 1b. It was identified that a slight size difference was obtained between TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) due to solvation effect. Therefore, such low dimensional particles would be potential for optical application.

The surface-functional groups of F-PNPs were explored by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak at 3240 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The characteristic peak at 1010 cm−1 (C–O) for hydroquinone disappeared in the spectrum of F-PNPs. The signal at 1650 cm−1 for C=N stretching vibration appeared, which verified the formation of C=N in F-PNPs. The above results indicated that Schiff base reaction between hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine resulted in the formation of such polymeric dots bearing C=N linkages.

The optical properties of F-PNPs were further recorded by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 , a major absorption band in the as-prepared F-PNPs can be clearly observed at about 288 nm, which can be assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Moreover, a relatively weak tail band of F-PNPs was located at 390 nm, which was ascribed to n-π* transition of C=N bonds [22]. The results also suggested that the C=N bonds of F-PNPs was formed and cross-linked with hydroquinone and polyethyleneimine. The excitation and emission spectra of F-PNPs were given and the peak maximum of F-PNPs was located at at 545 nm with excitation wavelength at 416 nm as described in Fig. 3. More importantly, the emission spectra follow a clear excitation-dependent feature. The F-PNPs can be excited by various excitation wavelengths ranging from 380 to 480 nm at 10 nm intervals with the emission peak wavelength from 523 nm (green) to 612 nm (red) as shown in Figure S2. In general, the excitation-dependent property would be attributed to chemical structure or the existence of diverse emissive traps on the F-PNPs surface [23–25]. In addition, the influence of pH on the fluorescent intensity was investigated. In Figure S3, the fluorescence intensity of F-PNPs gave rise to insignificant changes in the pH range from 8 to 14 and about 35% variation in the range of 3–7, indicating that this probe is relatively stable in a wide pH range for uses in biological imaging and environmental circumstances. To explore its stability within polar solvents, the emission spectra in water and buffer solution at different time period were recorded (Fig. S4). It was evident that the emission changes were not substantial since the time was extended to 48 h. The reduction in intensity was less than 5–10%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of SO3 2− in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the sensing behavior of F-PNPs, the emission spectra were measured by adding various concentration of SO3 2− with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm under optimal conditions. In the absence of SO3 2−, F-PNPs showed a green band at 545 nm. Upon addition of SO3 2−, a new peak at 425 nm emerged and the green emission peak at 545 nm was suppresed as shown in Fig. 4 a. To rule out the effect of pH and deterioration of sodium sulfite on the emission behavior in aqueous solution, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 14 and the pure sodium sulfate was added. The fluorescence profiles were measured in Fig. 4a and the curves maintained almost stable. The above results revealed that the addition of SO3 2− to F-PNPs solutions induced the luminescence change from green to blue.

To evaluate the photoluminescence properties of F-PNPs towards other ions, the sensing experiments of F-PNPs were performed in aqueous solution. The detection system with various ions including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2−, CO3 2–, PO4 3− and NO3 –) was studied by fluorescence spectra in parallel under the same condition. As can be seen in Figure S5, the emission speatra of the F-PNPs solution gave rise to negligible changes upon the addition of different species. Therefore, only the incorpoartion of SO3 2− would cause a new signal at 425 nm, indicating that F-PNPs was possible to be selected as a ratiometric probe to detect SO3 2− in aqueous solution.

With the purpose of investigating the sensitivity of F-PNPs for the detection of SO3 2−, the fluorescence titration experiments were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4b, the concentration of Na2SO3 was elevated from 0 μM to 50 μM, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm was increased gradually while the peak at 545 nm was decreased. Depending on the titration process, a preferable linearity (y = 1.05x-0.13, R2 = 0.9870) between the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (y-axis, F425/F545, F425 and F545 indicate the intensity of fluorescence emission at 425 nm and 545 nm) and the concentration of SO3 2− (x-axis) was observed in the range of 0–50 μM as shown in Fig. 4c. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as low as 59 nM according to the equation LOD = 3SD/K (SD refers to the standard deviation of blank measurements for ten times, K stands for the slope of the linear equation containing F425/F545 versus the SO3 2− concentration). The results corroborated that the sensitivity would be enough for the quantitative determination of SO3 2−. In order to study the reversibility of the probe, the green emission band could appear again with the addition of HCl in the sensing system of F-PNPs-SO3 2−. Based on this phenomenon in Fig. 4d, a (HCl and SO3 2−)-mediated reversible nano-switch can be realized to detect SO3 2−. In this way, this nanosystem could be governed via pH conditions. In addition to sensitivity and selectivity, a fast response time is desirable for real-time detecting purposes. In Figure S6, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm was improved as the incubation time was increased. Hence, the sensing platform could be completely switched on within 20 min and remained stable. The above information indicated such polymeric dots would be applied for specific and fast responses to SO3 2− and the recycling process could also be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to anions, cations also showed noteworthy effect on the photoluminescence properties. In this contribution, the binding of metal ions to functional groups on the surface of F-PNPs was explored. It was found that the addition of Cu2+ led to the reduction in emission intensity at 545 nm (Fig. 5 a). In the presence of Cu2+ at different concentrations from 0 μM to 70 μM (Fig. 5b), the emission band at 545 nm was decreased considerably. The value of F0-F (y-axis) follows a good linear relationship (y = 15.10x + 94.22, R2 = 0.9731) with the Cu2+ concentration (x-axis) from 0 to 55 μM (Fig. 5c). Based on the 3SD/K calculation, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 76 nM. The satisfactory result demonstrated that F-PNPs-based sensing system possessed a high sensitivity to detect Cu2+. Therefore, the specific selectivity of F-PNPs to the Cu2+ ions over other metal ions is essential when applied in environmental systems. As exhibited in Fig. 5d, only Cu2+ could cause fluorescence changes, whereas other metal ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Li+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+ and Pb2+), did not generate any significant changes under identical conditions. In addition, the time dependent fluorescence responses of F-PNPs toward Cu2+ were investigated. When Cu2+ was added, the emission peak at 545 nm decreased rapidly within 5 s and the fluorescence intensity would remain stable in the following 10 min as given in Figure S7.

To further improve the sensitivity and accuracy, we employed another effective emission band at 601 nm to detect Cu2+. It was noticed that the red peak could also be resposive to Cu2+ in Fig. 6 a. Since Cu2+ was added in the solution, the emission intensity was decreased with increasing Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 6b). As shown in Fig. 6c, a good linear relationship was obtained between the relative fluorescence intensity (y-axis, F0-F) and the Cu2+ concentrations (x-axis) in the range of 0 μM to 45 μM. The curve equation was interpreted as y = 20.67x-10.0 (R2 = 0.9951) and the limit of detection was determined to be 41 nM. Such value was analogous to the result (76 nM) of green band, which verified that the fluorescent probe had high sensitivity and accuracy. To evaluate the selectivity of F-PNPs sensor, the metal ions including Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Li+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+ and Pb2+ were measured in Fig. 6d. None of these potential interferences induced any apparent changes on the fluorescence spectra. These facts supported the polymeric dots can be used as a Cu2+ selective OFF-ON sensor. A time-dependent emission change was recorded and the intensity was turned off within 5 s (Figure S8). The fast response to Cu2+ would expand its practical potentials in real environments.

We have carried out the interference experiments. In the presence of both Cu2+ and SO3 2−, the emission band was only in responsive to sulfite upon the excitation wavelength at 370 nm (Fig. S9). When the excitation wavelength was changed to 416 nm, the emission profile was only sensitive to Cu2+ and the co-existence of sulfite would not interfere with the detection process (Fig. S9).

pH effects on the sensing experiments were performed (Table S1). It was found that in the relatively wide pH range from 4 to 12, the nanoparticles could determine the presence of Cu2+ or SO3 2− and the most sensitive condition was the neutral environment (pH = 7). Since Cu2+ and SO3 2− could react with OH– or H+, it should be mentioned that the optical probe had difficulty in sensing analytes when pH was less than 4 or higher than 12.

The quenching mechanism of F-PNPs in the presence of Cu2+ has been carried out, the decay curves of F-PNPs were obtained with the addition of Cu2+ in Fig. 7 a-b. For F-PNPs, the average lifetimes at 545 nm and 601 nm were mono-exponential with the value of 5.26 ns and 2.41 ns, which were basically the same as the data treated with Cu2+ (5.23 ns and 2.46 ns). These results suggested that the static quenching occurred between the surface of F-PNPs and Cu2+, owing to the ground state nonfluorescent complexation between luminophore and quencher. As provided in Fig. 7c, the average lifetime curves of F-PNPs in the presence and absence of SO3 2− were different, the fluorescence decay of F-PNPs at 425 nm treated with SO3 2− was 10.0 ns, which was longer than F-PNPs (5.26 ns). These results suggested that the dynamic quenching occurred between the surface of F-PNPs and SO3 2−. It might be caused by the collisional energy transfer between excited luminophore and the SO3 2−. Fluorescence quenching has been considered as a fundamental effect during recognition process. Both static and dynamic quenching are established on molecular-level contact between the nanoparticles and analytes. In the range of collisional (dynamic) quenching, the analyte should diffuse into the fluorophore accompanied by the change of excited state lifetime. There were no permanent changes in the molecular framework. However, in the case of static quenching, the “on–off” change could occur as a result of the formation of ground-state complex between the nanoparticle and guest species. The determination of fluorescence lifetimes would be the most reliable way to distinguish static and dynamic quenching. The lifetime measurement in static quenching could be maintained almost stable and the results were revealed in Fig. 7b and 7c. In contrast, the dynamic quenching was a typical time-dependent process as indicated by Fig. 7a.

To figure out the sensing process more clearly,the TEM images in the presence of Cu2+ were shown in Fig. 8 c. Compared with TEM images of F-PNPs in Fig. 8a, the morphology of F-PNPs in the presence of Cu2+ ion was completely different from original F-PNPs. The boundary for the particles became unclear. The results greatly supported that Cu2+ could induce agglomerations and the drastic structure change might lead to the emissionquenching. However, the TEM images in Fig. 8b demonstrated that the achieved F-PNPs were highly dispersed with an average diameter of 5 nm after the addition of SO3 2−. Such diameter was smaller than F-PNPs (average 6.2 nm). It is estimated that the blue emission caused by adding SO3 2− was assigned to quantum size effect [26].

Fluorescent signals are derived from contribution of intrinsic state emission and defect state emission [25]. Intially, the green band at 545 nm would be mianly ascribed to surface energy traps. But the encapsulation of reducing reagent (sulfite) changed the surface chemistry and suppressed non-radiative process by passivating the covering groups. As a result, the intrinsic state emission (blue band) acted as the major role in F-PNPs instead of defect state emission (green band).

In this case, the three dimensional space quantization is closely related to electron-hole pairs. Within nano-domains, the positive charge (hole) would be normally weakly interacted with electron over exciton Bohr radius. However, the addition of sulfite caused drastic morphological change and we could find evidence from the diameter evolution in TEM images (Fig. S10). The optoelectronic feature has been controlled by the particle size and shape. The smaller nanomaterial gives rise to shorter wavelengths [27]. The so called quantum confinement effect might occur since the individual nano-clusters were comparable or near to the exciton Bohr radius, The free movement of above-mentioned electron-hole pairs would be inhibited and the less delocalized electrons in the conduction band induced larger band gap with decreasing particle dimensions. The absorption edge for electronic transition of nanoclusters was moved towards higher energy level and blue shift was afforded (Fig. S10).

In the structure of an optical sensor, it contains two units including receptor and signal transduction moiety. Receptors chelated with metal ions and signal transduction part converted the chemical information into observable changes. In this work, the interaction of Cu2+ with the binding sites (NH, OH or C=N) would cause charge re-distribution process and the photoinduced electron transfer resulted in the emission quench. It was also observed that the band at 1650 cm−1 was shifted towards higher frequency (1658 cm−1), indicating that the chelation reaction between the nitrogen and Cu2+ would occur (Fig. S11). Moreover, the addition of Cu2+ generated serious aggregation of tiny nanoparticles (Fig. 8c). The spatial distribution of surface groups was in disorder and the emitting centers were located in close vicinity in aggregated phase. Therefore, the excited states of the nano-aggregates would easily decay in terms of non-radiative path ways and the formation of large particles with random shapes might lead to “off” state for the fluorescent signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of copper ions in laboratory tap water
ce:section.ce:para: It is highly expected that the polymeric dots could be applicable in practical aspects. Tap water samples were developed to verify the feasibility for visual detection of SO3 2− and Cu2+ in real sample. At the beginning, no target ions were monitored in the water samples. Therefore, the recovery test for the quantitation assay were carried out by analyzing samples spiked with three levels of target ions (1, 5, 10 μM). According to the above sensing way, the experimental results are presented in Table 1 . The results suggested that the average recoveries for intra-assay of SO3 2− reached 99.8~101.00% with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) ranging from 0.49% to 0.74%. In Table S2 and S3, the average recoveries for intra-assay of Cu2+ can be determined to be 98.00–102.50% with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) ranging from 0.32% to 1.02%. The collected results supported that the developed optical sensor with specificity might be value in the fields related to biomedicine study, environmental protection and food safety.

It is significant to develop new materials to determine SO3 2− or Cu2+ in aqueous solution since such ions were closely related to human health. We provided the one-step stirring method for the preparation of polymeric dots via polyethyleneimine (PEI) and hydroquinone (HQ) at low temperature. The as-prepared F-PNPs displayed superior aqueous dispersibility, excellent photo-and chemical stability and excitation wavelength dependence. Except the above properties, the ratiometric fluorescence response to SO3 2− was realized. In addition, the obtained F-PNPs possessed green emission at 545 nm and red emission at 601 nm due to excitation at 416 nm and 460 nm. Cu2+ could lead to the quenching effects at both 545 nm and 601 nm, thus providing a dual channel detection mode. To the best of our knowledge, it will be the first example for the ratiometric sensing of SO3 2− and double sensing modes of Cu2+. The new concept could be effective to design and assemble more nano-domains in bioanalytical fields.
Nengtao Wu: Writing - original draft. Qin Wen: Investigation. Qianming Wang: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q.M. thanks for the support from Guangdong International Science and Technology Cooperation Project (No. 2020A0505100055) and Guangzhou Science and Technology Plan (No. 202002030325).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119219.

ce:section.ce:para:
